title: The (Un)intended Impact of Patent Subsidy on Non-patenting Firms’ Innovation
speaker: Dandan Xia (Utrecht University School of Economics)
description:
Abstract:
We study whether patent subsidies stimulate innovation among non-patenting firms by facilitating knowledge spillovers. Leveraging the provincial roll-out of China’s patent-subsidy policy, we implement a staggered difference-in-differences design and find that non-patenting firms significantly increase new product sales after the policy is introduced. The effects are stronger for financially constrained firms, technological followers, and firms operating in discrete industries. Moreover, export data indicate that non-patenting firms’ product mix becomes more similar to that of patenting firms after the subsidy, consistent with disclosure-driven knowledge diffusion. We also document increases in overall sales and export volumes for non-patenting firms. Together, these findings highlight that patent subsidies generate positive externalities by facilitating information flows that enhance innovation beyond the direct recipients of the policy.
description_html:
Abstract:

We study whether patent subsidies stimulate innovation among non-patenting firms by facilitating knowledge spillovers. Leveraging the provincial roll-out of China’s patent-subsidy policy, we implement a staggered difference-in-differences design and find that non-patenting firms significantly increase new product sales after the policy is introduced. The effects are stronger for financially constrained firms, technological followers, and firms operating in discrete industries. Moreover, export data indicate that non-patenting firms’ product mix becomes more similar to that of patenting firms after the subsidy, consistent with disclosure-driven knowledge diffusion. We also document increases in overall sales and export volumes for non-patenting firms. Together, these findings highlight that patent subsidies generate positive externalities by facilitating information flows that enhance innovation beyond the direct recipients of the policy.

title: The Asking Price of Incorporation: State Leverage and the Evolution of Corporate Purpose
speaker: Fatjon Kaja
description:
Hybrid event.
To enter the virtual seminar room, please use the following login credentials:
Zoom URL:
https://uni-frankfurt.zoom-x.de/j/61215964575?pwd=kvOINlmRnzqDensLhFT385nGxtbuKJ.1
Meeting ID: 612 1596 4575
Password: 552446
Abstract:
This article advances a new perspective on corporate purpose, grounded in the institutional conditions under which corporate privileges are granted. Using a novel dataset of historical UK royal charters and a mixed-methods empirical strategy, the study shows that early corporations articulated specific, enforceable, and public facing purpose clauses because incorporation was a scarce privilege that allowed the Crown to impose obligations as a “asking price” for the benefits of the corporate form. Machine-learning evidence demonstrates that clauses reflecting Crown leverage cluster systematically and decline over time as incorporation becomes more accessible. The findings reframe corporate purpose not merely as a normative contest among stakeholders but as the product of institutional bargaining at the point of corporate formation, offering a historical lens for contemporary purpose debates.
Link to paper:
https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=6580099

Abstract:

This article advances a new perspective on corporate purpose, grounded in the institutional conditions under which corporate privileges are granted. Using a novel dataset of historical UK royal charters and a mixed-methods empirical strategy, the study shows that early corporations articulated specific, enforceable, and public facing purpose clauses because incorporation was a scarce privilege that allowed the Crown to impose obligations as a “asking price” for the benefits of the corporate form. Machine-learning evidence demonstrates that clauses reflecting Crown leverage cluster systematically and decline over time as incorporation becomes more accessible. The findings reframe corporate purpose not merely as a normative contest among stakeholders but as the product of institutional bargaining at the point of corporate formation, offering a historical lens for contemporary purpose debates.

title: Negotiating Parenthood: An Account of the Changing Relationship between Women's Work and Fertility
speaker: Fabian Kindermann (Universität Regensburg)
description:
Title:
Negotiating Parenthood: An Account of the Changing Relationship between Women's Work and Fertility
Abstract:
In high-income economies, the cross-country relationship between women's labor force participation and fertility has reversed in recent decades: countries where many women work used to have fewer children, but now they have more. In this paper, we examine the role of bargaining frictions in the household in accounting for this reversal. We develop a model in which partners bargain over how many children to have under limited commitment. Frictions arise because having children changes outside options. Bargaining frictions are large, and fertility is low, if mothers provide the majority of childcare while giving up lucrative career opportunities to raise children. In a simple setting, the bargaining friction is proportional to the marginal child penalty. When the only alternative for women is to raise children or to work, the relationship between women's labor supply and fertility is negative. When women's labor market opportunities are sufficiently attractive that market childcare is used, the relationship reverses. The model can account for the broad patterns in women's labor supply, child penalties, and fertility observed in high-income countries in recent decades.
description_html:
Title: 

Negotiating Parenthood: An Account of the Changing Relationship between Women's Work and Fertility

Abstract: 

In high-income economies, the cross-country relationship between women's labor force participation and fertility has reversed in recent decades: countries where many women work used to have fewer children, but now they have more. In this paper, we examine the role of bargaining frictions in the household in accounting for this reversal. We develop a model in which partners bargain over how many children to have under limited commitment. Frictions arise because having children changes outside options. Bargaining frictions are large, and fertility is low, if mothers provide the majority of childcare while giving up lucrative career opportunities to raise children. In a simple setting, the bargaining friction is proportional to the marginal child penalty. When the only alternative for women is to raise children or to work, the relationship between women's labor supply and fertility is negative. When women's labor market opportunities are sufficiently attractive that market childcare is used, the relationship reverses. The model can account for the broad patterns in women's labor supply, child penalties, and fertility observed in high-income countries in recent decades.

title: Spillover Effects Across Interactive Environments and Social Pollution
speaker: Eyal Winter (Hebrew University of Jerusalem)
description:
Title:
Spillover Effects Across Interactive Environments and Social Pollution (with Lihui Lin and Jiwei Zheng)
Abstract:
We study spillover effects across different interactive environments. Specifically, we examine how exposure to unfairness or exploitation in one environment affects individuals’ pro-social behavior in a completely separate environment. Our experimental design consists of two stages. In the first stage, participants face the possibility of exploitation; in the second, they play a battery of three games—the trust game, the public goods game, and the prisoner’s dilemma. Although participants in stage two interact with entirely new partners, experiences in stage one influence pro-social behavior in stage two for both the exploited and the exploiters.
We find that among the exploited, experiences of unfairness reduce their propensity to cooperate and to trust. Moreover, exploiters whose exploitation was unintentional also exhibit a decline in pro-social behavior. We interpret these results in the context of toxic workplaces, suggesting that workers’ experiences in such environments generate negative externalities that extend to interactions outside the workplace. These externalities can be viewed as a form of social pollution, which—like environmental pollution—should entail responsibility beyond the direct consequences for a firm’s own employees.
description_html:
Title: Spillover Effects Across Interactive Environments and Social Pollution (with Lihui Lin and Jiwei Zheng)

Abstract: We study spillover effects across different interactive environments. Specifically, we examine how exposure to unfairness or exploitation in one environment affects individuals’ pro-social behavior in a completely separate environment. Our experimental design consists of two stages. In the first stage, participants face the possibility of exploitation; in the second, they play a battery of three games—the trust game, the public goods game, and the prisoner’s dilemma. Although participants in stage two interact with entirely new partners, experiences in stage one influence pro-social behavior in stage two for both the exploited and the exploiters.

We find that among the exploited, experiences of unfairness reduce their propensity to cooperate and to trust. Moreover, exploiters whose exploitation was unintentional also exhibit a decline in pro-social behavior. We interpret these results in the context of toxic workplaces, suggesting that workers’ experiences in such environments generate negative externalities that extend to interactions outside the workplace. These externalities can be viewed as a form of social pollution, which—like environmental pollution—should entail responsibility beyond the direct consequences for a firm’s own employees.

title: The Impact of Personality Traits on Career Progression in Low-Skilled Labour Markets
speaker: Yingchao (Jane) Zhang (Durham University)
description:
Title:
The Impact of Personality Traits on Career Progression in Low-Skilled Labour Markets
Abstract:
This paper examines career progression out of low-skilled jobs in an overcrowded labour market using Australian panel data and Cox proportional hazards models. Among largely homogeneous-skill workers, we show that employers rely on personality traits to differentiate employees. Of the Big Five traits, extraversion is the only trait that significantly increases the likelihood of career progression, with no significant gender heterogeneity. The importance of extraversion has increased over time, consistent with the growing role of workplace collaboration.
description_html:
Title: The Impact of Personality Traits on Career Progression in Low-Skilled Labour Markets

Abstract: This paper examines career progression out of low-skilled jobs in an overcrowded labour market using Australian panel data and Cox proportional hazards models. Among largely homogeneous-skill workers, we show that employers rely on personality traits to differentiate employees. Of the Big Five traits, extraversion is the only trait that significantly increases the likelihood of career progression, with no significant gender heterogeneity. The importance of extraversion has increased over time, consistent with the growing role of workplace collaboration.

title: (Equivalized) Spending Changes at Widowhood
speaker: Bertrand Achou (University of Groningen)
description:
Abstract:
Using data from the Health and Retirement Study (HRS) and the Consumption and Activities Mail Survey (CAMS), we examine how widowhood affects spending—particularly equivalized spending—among US retirees. Applying standard equivalence scale values, we find that equivalized spending increases at widowhood. We then compare this finding to theoretical predictions about equivalized spending from different versions of the life cycle model, varying, for instance, in the ability to insure against widowhood risk. Through this analysis, we aim to determine the conditions under which such a phenomenon can arise and explore its potential implications for the adequacy of annuity and life insurance demand, as well as Social Security survivor benefits.
description_html:
Abstract: 

Using data from the Health and Retirement Study (HRS) and the Consumption and Activities Mail Survey (CAMS), we examine how widowhood affects spending—particularly equivalized spending—among US retirees. Applying standard equivalence scale values, we find that equivalized spending increases at widowhood. We then compare this finding to theoretical predictions about equivalized spending from different versions of the life cycle model, varying, for instance, in the ability to insure against widowhood risk. Through this analysis, we aim to determine the conditions under which such a phenomenon can arise and explore its potential implications for the adequacy of annuity and life insurance demand, as well as Social Security survivor benefits.

title: Capital Lock-in, Liquidity, the Separation of Ownership and Control
speaker: Giuseppe Dari-Mattiacci
description:
Hybrid event.
To enter the virtual seminar room, please use the following login credentials:
Zoom URL:
https://uni-frankfurt.zoom-x.de/j/62537088321?pwd=JvUBj2wqbZRlnXQmyoQlsbn06r11Fj.1
Meeting ID: 625 3708 8321
Password: 254890
Abstract:
Who should own firm assets, the collection of investors or a distinct legal entity? In a partnership, individual investors own firm assets and retain the right to unilaterally withdraw their capital at will. If, instead, firm assets are owned by a distinct legal entity (the corporation), investors implicitly waive this right, locking capital in the firm. Capital lock-in facilitates long-term investments but carries a risk of inefficient continuation of unprofitable projects. In contrast, withdrawal at will can lead to the inefficient liquidation of profitable projects. In this paper I provide a theory of capital lock-in and the choice of organizational form.

Abstract:

Who should own firm assets, the collection of investors or a distinct legal entity? In a partnership, individual investors own firm assets and retain the right to unilaterally withdraw their capital at will. If, instead, firm assets are owned by a distinct legal entity (the corporation), investors implicitly waive this right, locking capital in the firm. Capital lock-in facilitates long-term investments but carries a risk of inefficient continuation of unprofitable projects. In contrast, withdrawal at will can lead to the inefficient liquidation of profitable projects. In this paper I provide a theory of capital lock-in and the choice of organizational form.

title: Elicitability and identifiability of tail risk measures
speaker: Tobias Fissler (ETH Zurich)
description:
Abstract:
Tail risk measures are fully determined by the distribution of the underlying loss
beyond its quantile at a certain level, with Value-at-Risk and Expected Shortfall being
prime examples.
They are induced by law-based risk measures, called their generators, evaluated on
the tail distribution.
This presentation establishes joint identifiability and elicitability results of tail risk
measures together with the corresponding quantile, provided that their generators are
identifiable and elicitable, respectively.
As an example, we establish the joint identifiability and elicitability of the tail expectile
together with the quantile.
The corresponding consistent scores constitute a novel class of weighted scores,
nesting the known class of scores of Fissler and Ziegel for the Expected Shortfall
together with the quantile.
For statistical purposes, our results pave the way to easier model fitting for tail risk
measures via regression and the generalized method of moments, but also model
comparison and model validation in terms of established backtesting procedures.
Link to the full paper
description_html:
Abstract: 

Tail risk measures are fully determined by the distribution of the underlying loss beyond its quantile at a certain level, with Value-at-Risk and Expected Shortfall being prime examples. They are induced by law-based risk measures, called their generators, evaluated on the tail distribution. This presentation establishes joint identifiability and elicitability results of tail risk measures together with the corresponding quantile, provided that their generators are identifiable and elicitable, respectively. As an example, we establish the joint identifiability and elicitability of the tail expectile together with the quantile. The corresponding consistent scores constitute a novel class of weighted scores, nesting the known class of scores of Fissler and Ziegel for the Expected Shortfall together with the quantile. For statistical purposes, our results pave the way to easier model fitting for tail risk measures via regression and the generalized method of moments, but also model comparison and model validation in terms of established backtesting procedures.

 

Link to the full paper
